intro: C prioritizes hyper-optimized performance and procedural instruction sets over abstract readability, forcing full control over raw system allocations. Ruby shifts the architecture entirely to a dynamic, pure object-oriented scripting environment focused heavily on developer happiness and runtime flexibility. Moving from C to Ruby trading away raw processing velocity to gain massive acceleration in application design prototyping and text processing pipelines.

diffs.from: Static, unsafe types allowing arbitrary memory casting and pointer reinterpretation.
diffs.to: Dynamic, strong, object-oriented type system relying entirely on late binding and duck typing.

diffs.from: Rigid, brace-delimited imperative structure requiring meticulous layout declaration semantics.
diffs.to: Highly readable, flexible expression-driven layout omitting parenthetical requirements and explicitly terminating via `end` tags.

diffs.from: Explicit application-controlled manual heap layouts utilizing precise operational allocation points.
diffs.to: Automatic memory abstraction backed by a generational, mark-and-sweep garbage collection subsystem.

diffs.from: Manual multiplexing implementation against hardware flags or kernel thread libraries.
diffs.to: Thread abstractions constrained historical by a Global VM Lock (GVL), supplemented by modern Ractors and Fibers.

diffs.from: Minimal baseline utilities oriented toward raw storage, terminal standard streams, and mathematical logic.
diffs.to: Rich object library containing complex regular expressions, file systems drivers, network layers, and object introspection.

examples.title: File Reading Pipeline
examples.description: Simplifying raw low-level buffers into standard streaming object iterations.
examples.toCode:
def read_file
  File.foreach('data.txt') do |line|
    puts line
  end
end

examples.title: Dynamic List Storage
examples.description: Replacing complex variable array reallocations with abstract Array container objects.
examples.toCode:
# Arrays dynamically track capacity natively
arr = [10, 20]
arr << 30
# Garbage collector automatically cleans up allocation scope

tips.title: Everything in Ruby represents an Object
tips.body: Discard primitive scalar mindsets. In Ruby, literal integers (like `42`), strings, and classes are concrete object instances possessing functional methods.

tips.title: Embrace Dynamic Metaprogramming Features
tips.body: Instead of writing complex C macro generators, utilize Ruby tools like `method_missing` or `define_method` to modify runtime structural behaviors on the fly safely.

tips.title: Understand the Global VM Lock (GVL)
tips.body: Ruby threads run concurrently but are managed under a GVL framework that limits simultaneous execution of CPU-bound ruby instructions on multiple native cores.

tips.title: Utilize Native Extensions for Critical Performance
tips.body: If specific data algorithms demand C speed, write encapsulated Ruby C Extensions to expose fast compiled operations inside standard Ruby gems.
